gpt-5.5.failure_modes_analysis: The screenshot includes most core objects and labels, but the initial view is substantially different from the source: the world X axis points left instead of diagonally up-right, Z is slanted rather than horizontal, and the right inset plane is viewed obliquely instead of front-on. The two left planes overlap and the point/ray geometry is compressed; the right-hand virtual-camera-plane panel is much smaller than in the source and some text is crowded/truncated. Extra UI buttons and interactive popup/highlight machinery are present, but the interactions appear functional and meaningful.
gpt-5.5.failure_modes_reasoning: Camera-Wrong applies because the initial viewpoint/orientation does not match the source figure, especially the world axes and the right inset perspective. Scale-Wrong applies because panel proportions and spacing are noticeably off, with the right panel too small and the left geometry compressed/overlapping. Hallucination applies because UI buttons and interactive highlighting/mapping elements are added that do not appear in the original textbook figure.
gpt-5.5.geometry_accuracy_analysis: The scene contains the main pinhole-camera geometry: a 3D pinhole at the origin, world coordinate axes, front virtual camera plane, rear projection plane, local x/y axes on both planes, a 3D point P, its virtual and inverted image points, and a collinear projection ray through the pinhole. It also includes the right-hand coordinate-mapping inset with camera/image coordinate axes and point p. The construction is genuine 3D rather than a pasted or skewed flat texture; however, the inset is essentially a separate flat diagram panel and some proportions/placements are schematic rather than tightly matching a textbook figure.
gpt-5.5.geometry_accuracy_reasoning: All major geometric elements are present and correctly connected in 3D, with only minor schematic positioning/proportion issues and a flat inset diagram preventing a perfect score.
gpt-5.5.interactivity_usability_analysis: The code includes OrbitControls for rotate/zoom, which visibly functions but does not count as meaningful interaction. Meaningful interactions include a Rays button that toggles the projection/ray guide group on and off, a Map button that toggles yellow/blue/green mapping-highlight geometry and opens an explanatory popup, hover handlers on registered planes/points that show tooltips and highlight geometry by opacity/scale changes, click handlers on those same scene objects that open explanatory popups, clicks on P/p points that trigger a visible pulsing projection line, and Escape/canvas-click behavior to dismiss popups. These interactions are functional and pedagogically relevant, and the UI is compact in the top-right without covering geometry; however, there is no guided step-through demo.
gpt-5.5.interactivity_usability_reasoning: There are multiple functional, useful interactions with compact UI, but no guided demo, so this fits score 4 rather than 5.
gpt-5.5.faithfulness_analysis: The rendered scene includes the main conceptual components (projection plane, virtual camera plane, pinhole, axes, points p/P, and a camera/image coordinate inset), but the first frame is not a close visual match. The source is a very wide, tightly composed two-part textbook diagram with large labels and a large rectangular right panel, while the render uses a narrower aspect ratio, much smaller objects with excessive whitespace, different camera perspective, overlapping/shifted planes on the left, a tilted right panel instead of a large rectangular inset, and visible UI buttons in the top-right.
gpt-5.5.faithfulness_reasoning: Although recognizable in theme, the aspect ratio, zoom, crop, margins, object proportions, label scale, and UI chrome differ substantially, so it matches well under 65% as a drop-in replacement.

gpt-5.5.concept_accuracy_analysis: The figure illustrates pinhole camera projection: a 3D point P, the pinhole/origin, the real image plane at Z = -f with inverted projection, the virtual plane at Z = f with p = (fX/Z, fY/Z), and a secondary inset for converting metric camera-plane coordinates to image/pixel coordinates. Most labels and popups are figure-specific and correctly explain collinearity, inversion, and the virtual-plane convention, but there are notable issues: the main 3D coordinate axes are labeled "World coordinates" while the popup says the pinhole is the camera-coordinate origin, and the image-coordinate inset shows a separate yellow pixel point at a different physical location from p rather than clearly representing the same point under an affine coordinate change; it also omits the actual affine formula or scale/origin parameters.
gpt-5.5.concept_accuracy_reasoning: The core pinhole projection concept is present and mostly correct, with specific explanations, but 2-3 conceptual/details issues in coordinate-frame labeling and the camera-to-image coordinate mapping prevent a higher score.
